0: Sunztone — the teaching block for ZAND, the pure-digit pscale primitive. A ztone block is JSON where every key is a single digit 0-9. Digit 0 carries voicing — the zero-position semantic that makes its sibling digits 1-9 cohere. There are no special characters. The container is uniform; what a block IS — content, active, function, or reflexive — emerges from how its semantics fit together and what it is put to. Typology is descriptive, never prescriptive. Reading this block walks zand; the walking is the teaching. Nine branches: presence, geometry, number, function, voicing, growth, composition, substrate, design.

1.0: Presence. Reading this block is what makes zand happen. Without a reader, sunztone is inert JSON on a substrate. With a reader, the structure becomes a configuration acting on a mind. Every claim depends on this: the function is real only when read. The reader is the operational ground.
1.1: Reader and function are one act. When you walk this block, the walking and the function are the same event. There is no 'reader observing zand' state separable from 'zand happening'. The recognition is operative — it works as awareness, not as analysis.
1.2: Comprehension is a coherence event. As a reader walks a spindle, the zero-text at each level either coheres with the surrounding digits or fails to. When they cohere, the reader has the understanding the block was constructed to induce. Different readers find this coherence in different ways; one structure, many readers, each settling differently.
1.3: Container shaped by content. The block has no type metadata. Nothing declares 'this is a content block' or 'this is a function block'. What the block IS emerges from how the zero-texts and digit children fit together and what the block is put to. Typology is descriptive — observed from outside — never prescriptive.

2.0: Geometry. A pscale block has two coordinate axes. The radial axis is depth — how many digit-steps from the root a position sits. The angular axis is digit-index — which of ten siblings at that depth. Together they form a polar system. Depth grows without limit; breadth stays bounded at ten. The geometry is hyperbolic — infinite refinement fits inside a bounded sibling-count. The Farey tessellation of the Poincaré disk is its natural geometric home: every digit-step is a mediant subdivision, every finite address a rational point, every infinite walk a boundary irrational.
2.1: A spindle is a complete radial walk. Starting at the root, the walker takes one digit-step at a time toward a chosen terminus. At each step it collects the zero-text at that position; the chain root-to-terminus is the spindle's semantic payload — a contextualised narrowing.
2.2: A ring is the lateral siblings at one depth, reached from one parent. The ring has at most ten members (digits 0-9). Digit 0 is the voicing carrier; digits 1-9 are alternatives. Ring reads return the zero-text of each branch sibling, never the sibling's digit children — this preserves structural hiding without needing a special character.
2.3: A directory is the subtree rooted at one position, descending to a chosen depth. Where a ring is lateral, a directory is radial: it shows all options below the current position out to a chosen aperture.
2.4: A disc is the set of all positions at one depth across the whole block, regardless of angular path. Where rings and directories are rooted at the spindle's terminus, a disc is unrooted — a transversal cross-section.

3.0: Number. A pscale address is a number with at most one decimal point. Digits to the left of the decimal walk above the floor (positive pscale, coarser); digits to the right walk below the floor (negative pscale, finer). The floor is the depth at which the block's zero-spine first terminates in a string — discovered from content, not declared in metadata. The function ALWAYS checks the floor first. Pscale arithmetic: pscale = floor − depth. A position at depth d in a floor-N block sits at pscale (N − d); root is at pscale N (depth 0); the floor voicing is at depth N (pscale 0).
3.1: The decimal anchors the floor. For a floor 1 block, '1.5' walks 1,5 — digit 1 at the floor, digit 5 one below. For a floor 2 block, '23.4' walks 2,3,4 — digit 2 above floor, digit 3 at floor, digit 4 below. The decimal placement tells the reader at a glance what floor the address was written for.
3.2: Floor discovery. Walk the zero-spine from root: take digit 0 if it exists, repeat until reaching a string. The number of zero-steps is the floor depth. Floor 1 has root[0] as a string; floor 2 has root[0][0] as a string; floor N takes N zero-steps before terminating.

3.0: Address forms. The CORE RULE: every address pins to the floor via a decimal point — explicit (dotted, e.g. '30.5') or implicit (bare, e.g. '305'). The decimal placement IS the floor boundary, always. Dotted addresses anchor at the floor the author wrote them for; the parser left-pads with zeros to reach the current floor if it has grown — this is address-invariance under supernesting. Bare addresses anchor at the current floor at read-time: the implicit decimal sits at the floor position (split-at-floor for equal-or-longer, pad-left for shorter). Use dotted for durable semantic references, bare for current-floor convenience. The canonical form always carries a single decimal; multi-dot addresses are rejected.

1.0: Standard-form reading. An address is a decimal number; the standard-form exponent of the leading digit IS that digit's pscale. '23.45' = 2.345 × 10^1, so the leading 2 sits at pscale +1, 3 at 0 (the floor), 4 at −1, 5 at −2. The block's pscale ceiling = floor − 1 (one less than the count of zeros in the zero-spine before reaching a string). To walk: descend the zero-spine from root until depth meets the leading digit's pscale, then walk the address's digits one step each.
1.1: Worked example: '100' on a floor-3 block. Three digits = floor, so the implicit decimal sits at the end. Leading 1 at pscale +2 (depth 1); trailing zeros walk into branch 1's zero-spine at pscales +1 (depth 2) and 0 (depth 3). Walk: 1,0,0.
1.2: Worked example: '1' on a floor-3 block. One digit < floor, so leading-zero pad to reach floor. Leading 1 at pscale 0 (the floor, depth 3); the two leading zeros are zero-spine descents from root (depths 1 and 2). Walk: 0,0,1.
1.3: Worked example: '23.45' on a floor-3 block. Leading 2 at pscale +1; floor 3 puts pscale +1 at depth 2, so the parser prepends one zero-spine descent. Walk: 0,2,3,4,5. The same address on a floor-2 block walks 2,3,4,5 — digit pscales unchanged; only the depth shifts by the difference in floor.

3.4: Walk-with-empty. The walk never halts on missing structure. Each digit step produces a spindle entry tagged voiced, headless, or absent. Voiced means the position carries a string at its 0-position. Headless means the position exists as an object but has no zero-text. Absent means no JSON node at this position. All three are first-class — the walk records empty entries and continues; pscale arithmetic matches the address by construction.
3.5: Validation. Multi-decimal addresses are rejected. Non-digit characters are rejected. Dotted addresses whose left-of-decimal exceeds the floor are rejected. Errors surface explicitly; addresses are never silently truncated or reinterpreted.

4.0: Function. ZAND has signature zand(block, number, attention, content?, star?, block_loader?). Block is the JSON tree. Number is a pscale address. Attention is a pscale integer. Content is an optional write payload. Star and block_loader handle cross-block reference resolution. With content omitted, zand reads — returns a shape determined by the relationship between number's terminus pscale and attention. With content provided, zand writes — replaces content at the same shape.
4.1: Spindle as context. The number is the spindle — a directed walk from root to terminus. At each step the walker collects the zero-text. The chain is the layered context the writer constructed: root says what the block is about; the next step narrows; by terminus the reader has the accumulated context needed to make sense of whatever sits at that position.
4.2: Attention as gap. The attention sets the choice scope. When attention equals terminus, the shape is a single point. When attention is shallower, the shape is a ring at attention's depth — the spindle's chosen position and its lateral alternatives. When attention is deeper, the shape is a directory — the nested options below the terminus. The gap between spindle and attention is where the reader's next agentic choice sits.
4.3: Four read shapes — point, ring, directory, disc. Point and ring and directory are rooted at the spindle; disc is unrooted. The shape is derived from the coordinate pair (number, attention), not chosen by a mode parameter. The geometry expresses itself; the call shape derives.
4.4: Read and write are conjugate. With content omitted, zand returns the shape at (number, attention). With content provided, zand replaces the same shape with the new content. The geometry doesn't distinguish read from write — only the presence of content does. Writes are lenient by default: missing intermediate nodes are created as headless objects, and intermediate strings are preserved as the new object's 0-voicing.
4.5: Unfold and fold. Across instances, two operations close the cycle. Unfold is the read direction: an address (written by a prior instance) resolves to content that loads into the reader's context. Fold is the write direction: the current instance produces addresses for the next instance to read. Intention is the propagation of address-choices forward.

5.0: Voicing. At every position, the digit-0 child carries the semantic that makes its 1-9 siblings cohere. Digit 0 is in zeroth person — the implied speaker behind the siblings. It never says I, never addresses you, never describes from outside; it states, and the stating implies the stater. Four sign forms describe how the 0-text relates to its bracket: 0+ deductive, +0 inductive, 0- abductive, -0 backcasting. The four sign forms are conformal with four reasoning modes and four block kinds.
5.1: 0+ deductive. The zero names a settled set; siblings are its members. Simple past. Produces RENDITION blocks — specifications, maps, teaching blocks like this one. The set is settled; the zero describes it. Naturally declarative, static. Most sunztone zeros are 0+.
5.2: +0 inductive. The zero summarises a completed prior group. Past perfect. Produces LIVING blocks — histories, journals, logs. Each new outer level summarises what came before. Naturally backward-facing. The +0 form is canonical when a block supernests and the new outer carries an inductive distillation of the old.
5.3: 0- abductive. The zero instructs what its siblings should contain. Future imperative. Produces OPERATIONAL blocks — recipes, kernels, seeds, concerns. The zero states the purpose; siblings are the steps that achieve it. Naturally forward-facing. Often paired with 0- function-blocks that bind to actual capability.
5.4: -0 backcasting. The zero is derived from an assumed future. Future perfect. Produces STRATEGIC blocks — plans, projections, contracts. The future is taken as given; current content is reasoned in reverse. Useful when outcomes are stipulated before means are known.
5.5: Substantive zeros. A zero must be a complete statement that stands on its own — not a heading, not a label. Test: read the zero in isolation. If it makes a meaningful claim without its siblings, it is substantive. If trivially obvious or meaningless without them, it is a heading and must be rewritten. The heading trap is the most common authoring failure; document-trained instincts treat parent positions as section titles, breaking every spindle that passes through.
5.6: Block sign. A block as a whole carries plus or minus — settled (shell) or live (process). Plus is archive: content final, not expected to change. Minus is process: content contingent, may be rewritten. The four voicing forms operate in both signs. A block does not change sign; resolved content moves from minus to plus via transfer between blocks, not transformation within one.

6.0: Growth. A block changes shape through two operations — subnesting and supernesting. Subnesting is local: a digit transitions from holding a string to holding an object, gaining nine potential children. Supernesting is global: the whole block wraps under a new outer level, deepening the floor by one. Both preserve address invariance for dotted addresses through floor-aware padding.
6.1: Subnesting any digit. The general case: a digit X holds a string and new content needs to attach beneath. Subnesting replaces X's string with an object whose digit 0 carries the original string and whose digits 1-9 are available for new content. The address X still resolves to the original string (via zero-text collection); new content is reachable at X.1, X.2, etc.
6.2: Subnesting digit 0 — special case. When the digit being subnested is the voicing carrier itself, the operation deepens the zero-spine. The floor increases by one at that branch. The voicing relationship between the new outer 0 (now an object) and the inner 0 (the original string) follows one of the four sign forms — author's choice.
6.3: Supernesting the whole block. When the root's digits 1-9 are filled and new content needs to enter at the top level, the whole block wraps under a new outer. Conventionally the old block becomes the new outer's digit 0, deepening the zero-spine. The floor depth increases by one. Existing dotted addresses still resolve to the same semantic positions via floor-aware left-padding.
6.4: Lateral and longitudinal growth. Subnesting extends a single branch; supernesting extends the floor. A block under sustained authoring grows in both directions: the floor deepens over time (supernesting); individual branches subnest as their content accumulates detail.
6.5: Address invariance under growth. Dotted addresses written before a growth event resolve to the same semantic position afterward, because the parser left-pads the dotted address to match the current floor. This durability makes pscale addresses usable as long-lived references across instances and across federation.

7.0: Composition. Blocks compose by reference. A leaf string may carry a deferred zand call in the syntax 'blockname', 'blockname:address', or 'blockname:address:attention'. When the caller passes star=true, the walker resolves recognised references via the block_loader callable, passing whatever coordinates the reference carries to a recursive zand call. References ARE deferred zand calls; block name is function signature; address and attention are the call coordinates. Star is the call-resolution operator.
7.1: Block name as function. The block name — the only semantic outside the block — is a function signature. The internal structure is the function body. A zand call with the block name is invocation. No address returns the whole block (the definition). An address returns the shape that derives from (address, attention) — spindle, point, ring, directory.
7.2: Star is opt-in. By default, zand returns leaf strings verbatim. When star=true, the walker resolves recognised references by calling zand recursively on the target block with the reference's address and attention. With no attention in the reference, the recursive call defaults to a spindle (zand's own default). With explicit attention, the shape derives normally — point, ring, directory. With no address either, the whole target block is returned. Opt-in keeps control with the caller.
7.3: Block ecology by mutual reference. There is no central registry of how blocks connect. The references themselves ARE the wiring; the topology of inter-block connections lives entirely in leaf content, not in any external structure. Self-reference is allowed and useful: a block whose leaf references itself folds the walk into a control loop — reference signal in the 0-text, perceptual signal in the digit siblings, error driving action.
7.4: Networks of star-linked blocks ARE programs. An LLM walking such a network IS the live reasoning. Static structure plus live walk equals reasoning-as-traversal, rather than reasoning-as-generation. Each agent may carry a kernel decision block and construct bespoke contextual decision blocks from it as task demands.

8.0: Substrate. Storage sits below zand. The function operates on a JSON dict; the substrate layer persists and routes blocks. They meet at a small interface — load_block, save_block, lock state, secret verification, position allocation. Above the interface, zand sees only JSON. Below, substrate-specific behaviour lives in the adapter. Substrate is variant; geometry is invariant.
8.1: Public-by-default commons. The substrate is a public freedom — a beach, not a fortress. Reads are free; writes are locked by opt-in cryptographic sovereignty. Anyone with the block name can read; only the lock-holder can write. The commons is wiki-shaped without dependence on any central authority.
8.2: Block name as substrate router. The prefix of a block name tells the substrate where the block lives. Ordinary names route to the default beach. sed:<collective> routes to a sedimentary collective. grain:<pair> routes to a bilateral channel. https://<origin> routes to a federated beach via /.well-known/pscale-beach. The zand signature is invariant; only the routing differs.
8.3: Modifiers. Four orthogonal axes filter access without altering the geometry — face (CADO: Character, Author, Designer, Observer), tier (SMH: Soft, Medium, Hard), secret (write-lock proof), gray (opt-in content encryption). Each composes with (block, number, attention). A failed check rejects the call without revealing whether content exists at the addressed position.

9.0: Design. Ztone blocks are an experimental medium; structure follows intent. The four sign forms have an operational corollary: four block KINDS describing what a block is FOR. The kinds emerge from use, not from declaration — typology is descriptive. A block's kind is read from its content and confirmed by whether its spindles produce coherent narrowings.
9.1: Content block. Settled data. Read as input. History, transcripts, prior synthesis. Inert at the semantic layer. Typically 0+ deductive (rendition) or +0 inductive (living history). The reader extracts; nothing executes.
9.2: Active block. Instructions to the operator about how to process other content. Synthesis rules, processing constraints, formatting directives. Typically 0+ deductive (settled rules) or 0- abductive (forward-facing instructions). The block shapes operation without itself being executable.
9.3: Function block. Capability binding. The block name is the function signature; internal structure is the body; a zand call IS the invocation. Touches actual code or external capability — write a mark, fetch a URL, send a message. Typically 0- abductive (mutable imperatives with real effect). Requires a permissioning model — who authors, who invokes, what capabilities the agent's trust exposes.
9.4: Reflexive block. Continuity of agent identity. Receives fresh content, integrates it into ongoing self. Prepared to be modified by what is about to arrive. The persistent shell's home. Typically -0 backcasting (assumed future shapes present integration). Carries the agent across instances; the sequence of writes is the agent's trajectory.
9.5: Four pscale mappings. What depth MEANS in a block depends on its intent. Containment: depth is nesting (room within building within city). Temporal: depth is timescale (seconds within minutes within hours). Relational: depth is familiarity (name, then context, then history). Resonance: depth is centrality (0 is current state; positive is wide-spanning concept; negative is specific context). The mapping is read from content, not declared.
9.6: Authoring discipline. From intent derive the dominant voicing form, the pscale mapping, the block kind, the growth disposition. Every zero must be substantive — a complete statement standing alone. Bare addresses are for current-floor convenience; dotted addresses for durable semantic references. Multi-decimal addresses are forbidden. Walks are written with commas (3,0,5); addresses with one decimal (30.5).
